cues.subtitle: Valley Forge, Pennsylvania. Two days before Christmas, 1777. The British army is warm in Philadelphia, eighteen miles away. Washington's army — what remains of it — is here.
Twelve thousand men built this city in weeks: nearly two thousand log huts, fourteen feet by sixteen, twelve men to a hut. They built it because there was nowhere else to go.

The army is not starving because there is no food in Pennsylvania. It is starving because the systems to buy it and move it have collapsed. Some nights the camp's whole ration is firecake — flour and water scorched on a stone. In the dark, the huts chant it like a taunt: no meat. No meat.

A quarter of the army will die here — not in battle, but of typhus, dysentery, and exposure. Two thousand men, in a war whose battles rarely killed a tenth of that. Desertion runs constant. And yet the army holds together, all winter. Historians still argue about why.

In February a Prussian officer arrived — Baron von Steuben, lately of Frederick the Great's staff. He spoke no English. He drilled them anyway, a hundred men at a time, swearing in three languages. By spring, the survivors could maneuver like the professionals who had chased them out of New York.

The army marched out of Valley Forge in June, into a war it would not lose. Nothing was decided here. No battle was fought here. It is remembered anyway — because this is the place where the revolution was not abandoned.
